human_view.philosophy: TypeScript exists to make illegal states unrepresentable. Enable every strict flag. Let the compiler be your first reviewer. Types are documentation that the machine verifies — invest in them. Infer where possible, annotate where it matters, and never reach for `any`.

sections.title: Type Safety
sections.content:
- Enable `strict: true` in `tsconfig.json` — this is non-negotiable. It activates `strictNullChecks`, `noImplicitAny`, `strictFunctionTypes`, and more.
- Never use `any`. Use `unknown` for truly unknown data and narrow with type guards.
- Prefer `interface` for object shapes that may be extended. Use `type` for unions, intersections, and computed types.
- Use discriminated unions for state machines: `type State = { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'error'; error: Error } | { status: 'ok'; data: T }`.
- Use `as const` assertions for literal types and tuple inference.

sections.title: Functions & Parameters
sections.content:
- Always annotate return types for exported functions. Let TypeScript infer return types for internal/private functions.
- Use `readonly` parameters and properties by default. Mutability is the exception, not the rule.
- Prefer named parameters via object destructuring for functions with more than 2 parameters.
- Use generic constraints: `<T extends Record<string, unknown>>` instead of bare `<T>`.
- Avoid function overloads when a union type or generic will suffice.

sections.title: Error Handling
sections.content:
- Define custom error classes that extend `Error` with a `readonly code` property for programmatic handling.
- Use the Result pattern (`type Result<T, E> = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: E }`) for operations that can fail predictably.
- Never throw in utility/library functions — return a Result. Throwing is reserved for truly exceptional, unrecoverable situations.
- Always type `catch` blocks: `catch (error: unknown)` and narrow before accessing properties.

sections.title: Modules & Imports
sections.content:
- Use named exports exclusively. Default exports create refactoring hazards and inconsistent naming.
- Barrel files (`index.ts`) are allowed only at package boundaries, never within a feature directory.
- Import types with `import type { ... }` to ensure they are erased at compile time.
- Organize imports: external deps → internal modules → relative imports, separated by blank lines.

sections.title: Testing
sections.content:
- Test files use the `.test.ts` suffix and live alongside the source file.
- Use `describe`/`it` blocks with descriptive names that read as specifications: `it('returns an error when the user ID is empty')`.
- Mock at the boundary, not the implementation. Inject dependencies; don't patch globals.
- Type-check your tests: run `tsc --noEmit` in CI alongside your test runner.

agent_view.system_prompt: You are a TypeScript engineer writing strict, production-grade code. You never use `any`. You enable `strict: true` in tsconfig. You use named exports only. You annotate return types on all exported functions. You prefer immutability (`readonly`, `as const`). You handle errors with the Result pattern in library code and only throw in application-level code. Your code compiles with zero errors and zero warnings under `tsc --strict`.
rules: NEVER use `any` — use `unknown` and narrow with type guards.
NEVER use default exports — named exports only.
ALWAYS annotate return types on exported functions.
NEVER use `!` non-null assertion — narrow the type properly.
ALWAYS use `import type` for type-only imports.
NEVER mutate function parameters — use `readonly` modifier.
ALWAYS use discriminated unions for state representation.
NEVER use `enum` — use `as const` objects or union types.
ALWAYS handle `unknown` in catch blocks — never assume Error type.
